two-sum.explanation: Use a hash map to store each number and its index. For each element, check if its complement (target - num) exists in the map in O(1). Brute force is O(n²).

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.explanation: Track the minimum price seen so far and the maximum profit. One pass is sufficient. No stack or DP needed.

contains-duplicate.explanation: Add elements to a set and check for duplicates in O(1) per element. Sorting gives O(n log n) — the hash-set approach is strictly better.

valid-anagram.explanation: Count character frequencies with a fixed-size array of 26 integers (constant space for lowercase English). Sorting is O(n log n) and unnecessary.

group-anagrams.explanation: Use a hash map keyed by sorted string (or character frequency tuple). O(n * k) where k is max string length. Sorting every string individually without grouping is the brute-force trap.

top-k-frequent-elements.explanation: Count frequencies, then use bucket sort indexed by frequency (max n buckets). Heap approach gives O(n log k) — bucket sort achieves true O(n).

product-of-array-except-self.explanation: Compute prefix products left-to-right, then multiply by suffix products right-to-left in the same output array. No division, O(1) extra space (excluding output).

maximum-subarray.explanation: At each position, the max subarray ending here is max(nums[i], currentMax + nums[i]). One pass, constant space — Kadane's is O(n); divide-and-conquer is O(n log n).

longest-substring-without-repeating-char.explanation: Maintain a window with a set or map of characters. Expand right, shrink left when duplicate found. Each character is visited at most twice — O(n).

longest-repeating-character-replacement.explanation: Track the count of the most frequent character in the window. If window_size - maxCount > k, shrink from left. O(26) = O(1) space for the frequency map.

minimum-window-substring.explanation: Two-pointer sliding window with a character frequency map. Shrink from left when all required characters are satisfied. O(n + m) where n and m are string lengths.

valid-parentheses.explanation: Push open brackets onto a stack. For each closing bracket, check if the top of the stack is the matching open bracket. O(n) time and space.

trapping-rain-water.explanation: Use two pointers from each end. The shorter side determines water level at that position. Move the shorter pointer inward. O(1) space vs O(n) for the prefix-max-array approach.

number-of-1-bits.explanation: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit. Count how many times you can do this until n = 0. O(k) where k is the number of 1-bits — faster than checking all 32 bits.

counting-bits.explanation: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1). Shift right halves the number, which we've already computed. One pass, O(1) per element.

climbing-stairs.explanation: Only need the previous two values. Reduce O(n) space to O(1) by keeping two variables instead of the full DP array.

coin-change.explanation: Build a 1D DP table from 0 to amount. dp[i] = min(dp[i - coin] + 1) for each coin. Top-down memoization is equivalent but bottom-up avoids recursion overhead.

house-robber.explanation: Rob or skip. At each house, max_so_far = max(prev, prev_prev + nums[i]). Only two variables needed — O(1) space vs O(n) DP array.

longest-increasing-subsequence.explanation: Maintain a 'tails' array. For each number, binary search for the first tail >= nums[i] and replace it (or append). O(n log n) vs the classic O(n²) DP.

longest-common-subsequence.explanation: Classic 2D DP is O(m * n) space. Since each row only depends on the previous row, you can use two 1D arrays (or even one with a temp variable).

unique-paths.explanation: Each cell dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Only need the current and previous row — optimize to O(n) space. Mathematical combinatorics gives O(m+n) time O(1) space but DP is the intended pattern.

word-break.explanation: dp[i] = True if s[j:i] is in wordDict for some j < i where dp[j] is True. O(n²) positions * O(m) for dictionary lookup. Backtracking without memoization is exponential.

reverse-linked-list.explanation: Prev, curr, next. Iterative is O(1) space vs recursive O(n) call stack. Both are O(n) time.

merge-two-sorted-lists.explanation: Use a dummy node and move through both lists, appending the smaller element. O(1) extra space iteratively vs O(n + m) recursive call stack.

merge-k-sorted-lists.explanation: Push the head of each list into a min-heap. Pop the minimum, advance that list's pointer, push next. O(n log k) where n is total nodes and k is number of lists. Naive merge is O(nk).

binary-tree-level-order-traversal.explanation: BFS naturally gives level-order. Track level size before each BFS wave. O(n) time and O(n) space for the queue (max width at any level).

validate-binary-search-tree.explanation: Pass a valid range (min, max) through each recursive call. A node is valid only if it falls strictly within its range. O(h) space for recursion stack where h is tree height.

invert-binary-tree.explanation: Swap left and right children at every node. O(n) time visiting all nodes, O(h) space for the call stack where h is height (O(log n) balanced, O(n) worst case).

binary-tree-maximum-path-sum.technique: DFS returning max path through subtree
binary-tree-maximum-path-sum.explanation: For each node, compute the max path contribution from left/right subtrees (clamped at 0). Update a global max. O(n) — every node visited once.

number-of-islands.explanation: For each unvisited '1', DFS/BFS and mark all connected land as visited. O(m * n) time and space. Union-Find is also O(m * n α(m*n)) ≈ O(m * n).

course-schedule.explanation: Build adjacency list, then DFS with 3-color marking (unvisited/visiting/visited). If you encounter a gray (visiting) node, there's a cycle. O(V + E) for graph traversal.

implement-trie-prefix-tree.explanation: Each TrieNode stores children[26] for lowercase letters and a boolean isEnd. O(m) per insert/search/startsWith where m is word length.

find-median-from-data-stream.explanation: Maintain a max-heap of the lower half and a min-heap of the upper half, balanced within 1 element. Median is top of max-heap (or average of both tops).

insert-interval.explanation: Separate non-overlapping intervals before/after the new one. Merge all overlapping intervals in one pass. O(n) — no sorting needed since input is already sorted.

merge-intervals.explanation: Sort by start time. Merge current interval into result if it overlaps with the last merged interval. The O(n log n) sort dominates.

search-in-rotated-sorted-array.explanation: At each midpoint, determine which half is sorted. Narrow down which half the target belongs to. O(log n) — linear scan is O(n) and defeats the purpose.

container-with-most-water.explanation: Start with pointers at both ends. Move the shorter bar inward (since that's the only way to possibly find a taller bar). O(n) vs O(n²) for brute force.

3sum.explanation: Sort the array. Fix one element, then use two-pointer on the rest. Skip duplicates to avoid duplicate triplets. O(n²) — the O(n³) brute force is the common mistake.

jump-game.explanation: Track the furthest reachable index. If you reach an index beyond the current max reach, it's unreachable. O(n) — DP with memoization is also O(n) but O(n) space.

clone-graph.explanation: Use a hash map from original node to its clone. BFS/DFS and clone as you traverse. O(V + E) time and O(V) space for the map.

pacific-atlantic-water-flow.explanation: BFS inward from Pacific borders and Atlantic borders separately. Cells reachable from both are the answer. Reverse direction avoids checking all paths from each cell.

alien-dictionary.explanation: Build a character dependency graph from adjacent word pairs. Kahn's algorithm detects cycles (invalid input) and produces a valid ordering. O(C) where C is total characters across all words.
